title: Bi-directional ferrite to austenite transformation through warm temperature deformation of a ferrite-based low density steel
abstract_summary: The strain-induced transformation capability of a Fe-11.15Mn-5.6Al-0.07C low-density steel was investigated via warm compression tests (100–700 °C) and detailed microstructural analysis. The progressive development of banded structures led to simultaneous bi-directional ferrite-to-austenite and austenite-to-ferrite transformations, serving as a strain compensation mechanism and causing appreciable flow softening. Thermodynamic evaluation using a modified Gibbs energy model, combined with local elemental partitioning of Al and Mn, explained the nearly equal Gibbs energies of the constituent phases that enable the bidirectional transformation.
research_objective: To elucidate the mechanism and thermodynamic driving force for bi-directional deformation-induced ferrite-austenite transformation during warm temperature compression of a triplex ferrite-based low-density steel.
main_process_variables: hot rolling in 1100–1200 °C, 5 passes, air quenched
soaking at deformation temperature for 5 min

key_sections.role: Reviews deformation mechanisms in low-density steels and highlights the overlooked capability for dynamic bidirectional transformation, motivating the use of thermodynamic modelling.
Describes the processing route (ESR, homogenization, hot rolling, annealing) and experimental techniques (compression testing at 100–700 °C, SEM, EBSD, TEM, Thermo-Calc).

Presents initial triplex microstructure, flow curves showing softening at warm temperatures, microstructural evidence of banded structure and bidirectional transformation, and thermodynamic analysis of Gibbs energy vs. Al/Mn partitioning.
Summarizes the bi-directional transformation mechanism, its relation to Al/Mn elemental partitioning, and the equal Gibbs energies of ferrite and austenite that enable strain compensation.
